2 min read
1 hour ago

--

One of the biggest myths in React is:
“A component only re-renders when its own state changes.”
Not quite.
Understanding why React re-renders components is one of the biggest differences between a junior and a senior React developer.
Let’s break it down.

What is a Re-render?
A re-render simply means React calls your component function again to determine what the UI should look like.
It doesn’t mean the browser redraws the entire page.
React compares the new Virtual DOM with the previous one and updates only the parts of the real DOM that have changed.

When Does React Re-render?
1. State Changes
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);setCount(count + 1);Updating the state triggers a re-render.

2. Parent Component Re-renders
function Parent() {
return <Child />;
}If Parent re-renders, Child Also re-renders by default—even if its props haven't changed.
This surprises many developers.

3. Props Change
<Profile name={user.name} />When props change, React re-renders the component with the new values.

4. Context Value Changes
Using useContext means your component re-renders whenever the context value changes.
This is why it’s important to avoid putting frequently changing values into a global context unless necessary.

What Doesn’t Cause a Re-render?
Updating a Ref
const inputRef = useRef();inputRef.current = "Hello";Changing a ref does not trigger a re-render.
Refs are useful for storing values that shouldn’t affect the UI.

Should You Stop Every Re-render?
No.
Re-renders are a normal part of React.
The goal isn’t to eliminate them — it’s to avoid expensive, unnecessary ones.
Premature optimization often makes code harder to maintain.

Practical Tips

• Keep state as local as possible.

• Use React.memo Only when profiling shows a benefit.

• Avoid creating new objects or functions unnecessarily in props.

• Split large components into smaller ones.

• Use the React DevTools Profiler to measure performance before optimizing.

Key Takeaway
Re-renders are not the enemy.
The real challenge is understanding when they happen, why they happen, and whether they actually impact performance.
Measure first. Optimize second.

Discussion
What’s the biggest misconception about React re-renders you’ve encountered?
I’d love to hear your thoughts and experiences.
